title: Chinese window grille papercut
subtitle: Cutting a geometric lattice pattern in red paper using the fold-and-cut technique from the jianzhi tradition
excerpt: Chinese window grille papercuts are geometric lattice designs, traditionally cut in single red sheets and placed on windows for festivals. The fold-and-cut approach makes them accessible even to beginners: folding the paper before cutting means a single cut produces multiple identical shapes, and the symmetry of the finished design is built into the folding, not the cutting. A basic four-fold sheet produces an eight-fold symmetric design.
sourceNotes: Jianzhi is one of the oldest paper art forms in the world; the fold-and-cut technique for window grille patterns (chuānghuā) is part of the Chinese folk art tradition. General documentation in: Amy Uyeki, Asian Papercraft (2003) and standard museum collections of folk papercuts.

glossaryTerms.definition: Chinese paper-cutting, practised for over 1,500 years. Traditional jianzhi uses red or gold paper and scissors (not a knife) to create decorative motifs for windows, lanterns, and celebration. The window grille pattern (chuānghuā) is one of the most recognisable sub-types.

A technique in which the paper is folded before cutting so that the cut is mirrored across the fold line. A single fold produces bilateral symmetry; four folds produce eight-fold rotational symmetry. All symmetrical papercut designs use some variant of this principle.

The areas of a papercut that are removed. In a window grille design, the negative space is the open lattice through which light passes; the positive space is the paper framework that holds the lattice together. The design relies on the negative space being at least as large as the positive space, or the paper will be too fragile to hold its shape.

content.text: window grille patterns are designed so that the

forms an open lattice. Unlike silhouette cuts where the removed areas are background, in a window grille the cut-away areas are the design, the light comes through the holes. This means the positive paper framework must form a continuous connected structure; if any element is surrounded entirely by cuts, it will fall out.

items.name: Light box, A4 (for tracing folded pattern)

content.text: Start with a 15 × 15 cm square of tissue or lightweight red paper. Use the

method: fold in half horizontally, then in half vertically, producing a 7.5 × 7.5 cm square. Fold diagonally once more to produce a triangle. The resulting eight-layer triangle is one-eighth of the final design; every cut is reproduced eight times when the sheet is unfolded.
Drawing and cutting the grille pattern

On the folded triangle, draw a simple geometric grid: parallel lines 5 mm apart from the outer edges toward the centre, with cross-cuts between them. All lines must stop 3-4 mm before the fold edges, the fold edge becomes the connecting framework when unfolded.
Cut the interior shapes first, working from the centre outward. Use the craft knife on the cutting mat for the interior geometric cuts. Use scissors for any curved outer edges.
Unfold very carefully, wet or damp fingers help, from one fold at a time. Lay the unfolded cut flat and press gently with a ruler to remove the fold creases.

items.symptom: Cut sections fall out when the sheet is unfolded
items.cause: Cut lines reached all the way to the fold edge, severing a connection point.
items.fix: Draw a 3 mm margin inside every fold edge before cutting and never cut across it. The fold edges become the structural borders of the final design.

items.symptom: The paper tears at the fold lines when unfolding
items.cause: Paper too thin and the multiple fold layers have weakened at the fold.
items.fix: Unfold one layer at a time, working slowly and pressing each layer flat before moving to the next. Paper at 60-70 gsm handles the eight-layer fold better than very thin tissue.
